docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This Serverless project is similar to serverless-qa-oblt, but without apps deployed. edge-benchmarks is used to run the benchmarks for the edge versions.

docs.title: Update
docs.text: The update of the clusters is automatic when the QA environment push a new version of the stack. The version to update is the latest stable version of the latest on the QA environment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Serverless k8s is a GKE cluster to shared by all the serverless projects. Every pod deployed on the serverless projects will be deployed on this cluster.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Serverless-production-oblt is an observability test cluster deployed using the latest serverless version deployed in the Production environment. The aim of serverless-production-oblt is to evaluate the observability features in a serverless environment. The deployment is made following the instructions at create a Production deployment The serverless-production-oblt deployment has two pieces, one is the Elastic Stack and a Kubernetes(k8s) cluster.

The Elastic Stack is deployed on the Production environment, the deployment uses the Elastic Stack available on the Production environment at the time of the creation, the deployment is created every day so the SNAPSHOT version could have 24 hours.

The k8s cluster is deployed on Google Kubernetes Engine(GKE) Autopilot, this k8s cluster is monitored with The Elastic Agent running the Kubernetes integration. In the k8s cluster we deploy some applications and services in order to generate data (logs, metrics, and APM). The environment will composed by the following services:

  • Fleet Server
  • Elastic Agent
  • APM server managed
  • Opbeans application

CCS is not supported it is not possible to attach developers clusters if you want a developer cluster to work on serverless you have to use the serverless template to deploy one see Serverless deployments

docs.title: Access the cluster
docs.text:
The serverless project has only the ESS SSO enabled, and it is impossible to configure a different Realm for authentication. Because of that, the only way to access the deployed cluster is to use the ESS SSO. To access any cluster deployed with oblt-cli you must be a member of the robots organization, you can self-enroll to the robots organization either typing /oblt-onboarding-cloud in the observablt-robots Slack channel or filling this GitHub issue.

We have a shared user to access the QA and staging environments if you cannot change organizations.

Note

The shared user is only for internal use. We do not have a shared user for the production organization.

docs.title: oblt-robot
docs.text: To retrieve the credentials of the cluster you can use the oblt-robot Slack bot. It is installed in the slack channels: observablt-robots, #observablt-bots, and a few others. To ask the bot for the credentials you should write the Slack command /cluster-secret and choose the cluster you want to obtain the credentials.

docs.title: oblt-cli
docs.text:
To obtain the credentials of the cluster you can use the following command.

oblt-cli cluster secrets credentials --cluster-name serverless-production-oblt

docs.title: Update
docs.text: The update of the clusters is automatic when the Production environment push a new version of the stack. The version to update is the latest stable version of the latest on the Production environment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The aim of serverless-production-sec is to evaluate Security features in a serverless environment. The project is created in the Production environment and therefore its version is kept up-to-date with the latest deployed there.

CCS is not supported it is not possible to attach developers clusters if you want a developer cluster to work on serverless you have to use the serverless template to deploy one see Serverless deployments

docs.title: Access the cluster
docs.text:
The serverless project has only the ESS SSO enabled, and it is impossible to configure a different Realm for authentication. Because of that, the only way to access the deployed cluster is to use the ESS SSO. To access any cluster deployed with oblt-cli you must be a member of the robots organization, you can self-enroll to the robots organization either typing /oblt-onboarding-cloud in the observablt-robots Slack channel or filling this GitHub issue.

We have a shared user to access the QA and staging environments if you cannot change organizations.

Note

The shared user is only for internal use. We do not have a shared user for the production organization.

docs.title: oblt-robot
docs.text: To retrieve the credentials of the cluster you can use the oblt-robot Slack bot. It is installed in the slack channels: observablt-robots, #observablt-bots, and a few others. To ask the bot for the credentials you should write the Slack command /cluster-secret and choose the cluster you want to obtain the credentials.

docs.title: oblt-cli
docs.text:
To obtain the credentials of the cluster you can use the following command.

oblt-cli cluster secrets credentials --cluster-name serverless-production-sec

docs.title: Cluster info
docs.text: Elastic Stack (deployed at 2024-07-24 15:07:09)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
synth-sec is a long running ESS cluster deployed in the Cloud First test region in production. It uses the latest SNAPSHOT, therefore it is unstable and contains the features of the current development branch. The ESS deployment has Elasticsearch, Kibana, and Integrations server.

For more details about how to use it and implementation check the Documentation Site

docs.title: Kind of data
docs.text:
Synthetic data generated for specific purposes. Currently data is generated on an ad hoc basis into specified indices within a specified date range. The goal is to have regular generation of data so data is consistently indexed that is close to the current date.

A data set generated for the Security Explore dashboards: Network, Users and Hosts.

  • Index: logs-synthetic-explore-default
  • Date Range: 2024-05-01 to 2024-05-30
  • Documents: 10000000
  • Specification: https://github.com/elastic/guts/blob/main/scripts/gen_synth/document_spec/explore.yml

docs.title: Data Caching
docs.text: Elasticsearch will cache results to improve performance. This will result in very low query response times when queries are repeated, documented here https://github.com/elastic/guts/issues/44. Clearing the cache before tests is good practice and can be done using the elasticsearch API /_cache/clear.

docs.title: Update
docs.text: The update is automatic and happens on every Tuesday. The version to update is the latest snapshot. Developers clusters are also automatically updated on every update of the cluster.

docs.title: URLs and Credentials
docs.text:
You can check the URLs, user and passwords needed to access any of services in the following URLs

  • Credentials and URLs
  • Elasticsearch
  • APM
  • Kibana

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
synthetics-billing-audit is a long term ESS cluster deployed in the Cloud First test region in production. We use the latest release or current BC, therefore it is stable and contains the features of the current release or Build Candidate (BC). The ESS deployment has Elasticsearch, Kibana, and Integrations server.

The Elastic Stack is configured with Stack Monitoring and APM reporting to monitoring-oblt

For more details about how to use it and implementation check the Documentation Site

docs.title: Update
docs.text: The update of the clusters is automatic and it happens every Tuesday. The version to update is the latest stable version of the next release Elastic Stack, this include Build Candidates versions(BC). Developers clusters are also automatically updated on every update of the cluster.

docs.title: Release oblt repo and tools
docs.text: We have a release process for the oblt repo and tools. The release process will release a new version of the oblt repo and tools every time the .ci/version is modified on the main branch. The release process has the following cases:

docs.title: Manual release
docs.text:
There is a workflow name Release, this workflow allows you to select the type of release to make, then will bump the version and make a PR.

  • Go to GitHub Actions, to the workflow Release
  • Run the workflow selecting the type of release [major, minor, patch]
  • Wait for the workflow to finish
  • Check the PRs for a PR named Bump version X.Y.Z
  • Check that everything is fine and merge the PR
  • The workflow release-tools is triggered
  • It creates a new GitHub Release with release notes
  • It builds and uploads the binaries to the release.

You can use the same steps the CI use, the file .ci/Makefile has all the target needed. To check the targets available you can use make -C .ci help. The command make -C .ci release-patch perform a patch release. The command make -C .ci release-minor perform a minor release. The command make -C .ci release-major perform a major release.

docs.title: Release after a PR is merged
docs.text:
The workflow release-tools is triggered also when a PR contains changes on .ci/version, so to perform a release after merge a PR, it is enough to bump the version of the file .ci/version, then push the changes to your PR, and finally when the PR is merged a new release would be performed.

  • Make your changes on the repo
  • Update .ci/version to a new version
  • In the project root dir run: make -C tools/oblt-cli build and then make -C tools/oblt-robot build
  • Create a PR
  • Check all your changes are correct
  • Merge the PR
  • The workflow release-tools is triggered
  • It creates a new GitHub Release with release notes
  • It builds and uploads the binaries to the release.

docs.title: Oblt-cli
docs.text:
The oblt-cli tool is a multi-purpose tools that:

  • allows anyone with access to the observability test environments repository and Git configured to create/update/destroy oblt clusters configured for CCS.
  • queries the Unified Release process to gather what are the current releases, snapshots or active branches.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: The oblt-cli binaries are published as packages at the observability test environment releases.

docs.title: macOS via Homebrew
docs.text:
Homebrew is the easiest way to install oblt-cli on macOS. See the Homebrew documentation for installation and usage explanations.

NOTE: Since v6.3.1

First configure the oblt-cli third-party repository (called a "tap") with:

export HOMEBREW_GITHUB_API_TOKEN=$(gh auth token)
brew tap elastic/oblt-cli

or if you have git setup to default over SSH:

env HOMEBREW_GITHUB_API_TOKEN=(gh auth token) brew tap elastic/oblt-cli git@github.com:elastic/homebrew-oblt-cli

Then proceed with the actual installation with: brew install elastic/oblt-cli/oblt-cli

docs.title: Manual process
docs.text:
You have to uncompress the package and copy the oblt-cli binary in a folder inside your PATH.

NOTE: macOS users will have to manually verify the binary using this command:

xattr -r -d com.apple.quarantine oblt-cli

docs.title: Go way
docs.text:
If you have Go installed in your machine you can install the tool using the following command:

GOPRIVATE=github.com/elastic go install github.com/elastic/observability-test-environments/tools/oblt-cli@latest

docs.title: Configure
docs.text:
In order to use the oblt-cli tool it is needed to configure a couple of values used by all the commands, the following command will create a configuration folder ~/.oblt-cli and a config file ~/.oblt-cli/config.yaml with the settings passed as parameters.

Warning

The Slack member ID require to add a @ before the Slack member ID, for example @MYSLACKMEMBERID.

oblt-cli configure --slack-channel=@MYSLACKMEMBERID --username=my_github_username

or

oblt-cli configure --interactive

NOTE The Slack member ID is required, the Slack display name does not work in some cases. To grab your Slack member ID you have to go to your profile an copy it.

By default oblt-cli uses SSH to perform Git operations, it is the recommended way, however, HTTP is supported. To configure oblt-cli to use HTTP use the flag --git-http-mode in the configure command:

oblt-cli configure --slack-channel=@MYSLACKMEMBERID --username=myusername --git-http-mode

To perform git operations using HTTP a valid GitHub access token and Caching your GitHub credentials in Git is needed or alternatively: Updating credentials from the macOS Keychain

docs.title: Github credentials problem
docs.text:
Github does not support authentication via username:password anymore. So if you are asked to enter your github username when running any oblt-cli command, you first need to follow the steps to create a new GitHub access token (select all repo options).

Then allow the newly generated token to access the Elastic organisation, by clicking the Enable SSO button, and then selecting Authorize and follow through the steps.

With the new GH access token in hands run:

$ oblt-cli version
...
$ Username for 'https://github.com': <GH_USER_NAME>
$ Password for 'https://<GH_USER_NAME>@github.com': <GH_ACCESS_TOKEN>
...
$ oblt-cli version X.Y.Z

Once you've done that, you should never have to enter you GitHub credentials when running any oblt-cli command again, unless they have expired.

docs.title: Clean configuration
docs.text:
If you want to clean the configuration, you can remove the ~/.oblt-cli folder. It will remove the configuration file and the logs. So in order to keep the configuration file, and clean the ~/.oblt-cli folder, you can use the following command:

oblt-cli clean

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
It follows the format entity -> verb/ entity -> sub-entity (-> verb)?

Command What bootstrap Command to bootstrap Elasticsearch or Kibana using known recipes. ci Command to operate with the CI. cluster Command to operate a cluster. completion generate the autocompletion script for the specified shell configure It configures the oblt-cli. help Help about any command unified-release Command to query the Unified Release. update Command to self-update the tool. version Command to show tool's version.

There are some flags that can help to add verbose output or dry run:

Flags:
      --config string        config file (default is ${HOME}/.oblt-cli/config.yaml)
--dry-run              If true, the Git changes will not commit the changes made to the Git repository.
      --experimental         If true, the experimental features will be available.
  -h, --help                 help for oblt-cli
      --output-file string   It is the absolute path to a file to save the operation output results.
      --verbose              If true, the logs output is more verbose.

You can easily see what each command does by running oblt-cli [command] --help.

For more info about all command available check oblt-cli command reference

docs.title: List clusters
docs.text:
It is possible to list the clusters deployed by the user by running

oblt-cli cluster list

This list the configurations available for a user. The fact that the configuration exists does not mean the cluster is up an running.

There are more clusters configuration than the current user configuration, the following command lists all available clusters.

oblt-cli cluster list --all

docs.title: Create CCS cluster
docs.text:
Once we have configured our general settings we can create a new cluster configured for CCS and received the credentials in slack, to do that we can use the following command.

oblt-cli cluster create ccs --remote-cluster=edge-oblt

This command will push a commit to the observability test environments repository, that commits triggers a build that takes about 5 min (https://apm-ci.elastic.co/job/apm-shared/job/oblt-test-env/job/manage-user-oblt-clusters/) when the build ends it will send a slack message with the result and the credentials (if it succeeded).

NOTE: The cluster-name parameter is the oblt cluster configured as a remote cluster for CCS, not a unique name for the cluster. The edge-oblt name, for example, will deploy this oblt cluster.

docs.title: Read secrets
docs.text:
When a cluster is created, certain secrets are created in Vault including useful information for the end users. In the past, those secrets were posted as Slack messages, which made it difficult to retrieve them again after a period of time without searching/scrolling up in the Slack channel.

Besides those Slack messages, it's possible to retrieve those Vault secrets from a cluster using this tool. The tool provides three handy subcommands which allow to read three specific secrets from a cluster. These subcommands need the mandatory --cluster-name flag to select the cluster where to read the secret from.

Read kibana's config file from a cluster:

oblt-cli cluster secrets kibana-config --cluster-name=my-cluster

Read users for a cluster:

oblt-cli cluster secrets users --cluster-name=my-cluster

Read deployment information from a cluster:

oblt-cli cluster secrets info --cluster-name=my-cluster

Get deployment all details in YAML

oblt-cli cluster secrets digest --cluster-name=my-cluster

docs.title: Reading all secrets
docs.text:
The tool provides an advanced mode which allows to list the Vault secrets from a cluster, prompting the user to select them from a list. Once selected, the secret will be posted as text in the terminal. This advanced mode is implemented under the cluster secrets command, which needs the mandatory --cluster-name flag to select the cluster where to read its secrets from.

oblt-cli cluster secrets --cluster-name=my-cluster

docs.title: Destroy a cluster
docs.text:
It is possible to destroy a cluster using the name to identified it.

oblt-cli cluster destroy --cluster-name=my-cluster

NOTE this removes the configuration from the repository, the the CI job destroy the cluster.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
If you are interested in contributing to this project, please see our contributing guide.

Made with ♥️ and ☕️ by 🤖.

docs.title: Build and Test
docs.text:
Oblt-cli is a command line tool develop in Go, the requirements to compile it are Go 1.15+ and make installed in the machine. All the generated files are copied to a build folder inside the project.

git clone git@github.com:elastic/observability-test-environments.git
cd observability-test-environments
git checkout ccs_deployment_2
cd tools/oblt-cli
make clean lint build test

To install the tool in your Go bin folder you can use go install

NOTE tests are a work in progress, there are 2 that failed, even though. the feature works (manual tested).

docs.title: Add a new command
docs.text:
If you'd like to extend this tool, please use below standard:

oblt-cli [entity]+ [verb]? [flags]*

docs.title: Bootstrap Elasticsearch and Kibana
docs.text: There are many configuration which we share across developers. We have unified some of those shared configuration in oblt-cli to make it possible to run some recipes to initialize and configure settings in Elasticsearch and Kibana.

docs.title: List bootstrap recipes
docs.text:
This command will list all the recipes available for Elasticsearch and Kibana.

oblt-cli bootstrap list

docs.title: Apply recipes to a cluster
docs.text:
The easiest way to bootstrap an oblt cluster is using this command, you only have to pass the name of the cluster to apply all the recipes.

oblt-cli bootstrap cluster --cluster-name edge-oblt

In the case you only want to run some recipes it is possible to choose which recipes to apply to Elasticsearch and Kibana

oblt-cli bootstrap cluster --cluster-name edge-oblt --recipes-elasticsearch '["test"]'
oblt-cli bootstrap cluster --cluster-name edge-oblt --recipes-kibana '["test"]'

docs.title: Apply recipes to a Elasticsearch instance
docs.text:
In some cases we want to initialize an Elasticsearch that is not part of a oblt cluster, for this task we have to run oblt-cli bootstrap elasticsearch command. You need to pass the Elastisearch URL and a method to authenticate.

oblt-cli bootstrap elasticsearch --url https://es.example.com --username elastic --password changeme

In the case you only want to run some recipes it is possible to choose which recipes to apply to Elasticsearch

oblt-cli bootstrap elasticsearch --url https://es.example.com --username elastic --password changeme --recipes '["test"]'

docs.title: Apply recipes to a Kibana instance
docs.text:
In some cases we want to initialize a Kibana that is not part of a oblt cluster, for this task we have to run oblt-cli bootstrap kibana command. You need to pass the Kibana URL and a method to authenticate.

oblt-cli bootstrap kibana --url https://kibana.example.com --username elastic --password changeme

In the case you only want to run some recipes it is possible to choose which recipes to apply to Elasticsearch

oblt-cli bootstrap kibana --url https://kibana.example.com --username elastic --password changeme --recipes '["test"]'

docs.title: Add your own recipe
docs.text:
The recipes are simple YAML files to describe a REST API call. The recipes are in the observability-test-environments repo in the folder deployments/bootstrap. They are two types of recipes, for Elasticsearch at deployments/bootstrap/elasticsearch, and for Kibana at deployments/bootstrap/kibana. The structure of a recipe file is the following:

---
# Description of what the recipe does.
description: Basic test request
# Path to the REST API call.
api: "/"
#HTTP method to use it can be GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, ...
method: GET
# HTTP header we need to pass.
headers:
Content-Type: application/json
kbn-xsrf: true
# The payload to send to the REST API.
body: ""
# The expected return code.
return_code: 200

A new file in those folders is a new recipe available to use by everyone.

docs.title: End Of Life (EOL)
docs.text:
We are committed to providing support for our tools. We make frequent releases to include new features, bug fixes, and security updates.

Oblt-cli use semantic versioning, Major.Minor.Patch. Minor and Patch releases are backward compatible, so no breaking changes are introduced. Major releases may introduce breaking changes, and we will provide migration guides and deprecation warnings. Every time we release a major version, we stop supporting the previous major version, and the update is mandatory. Users that try to use oblt-cli in a previous major version will receive an error message and a link to the migration guide, the tool stop working for them until they update.

The reason for this is to keep the codebase clean and maintainable, and to avoid technical debt. Our tooling is focused on our daily work, and the update to the new version is simple and fast. There is no reason that blocks developers to update to the latest version.

docs.title: Local Kibana instance
docs.text:
When the process to create the CCS cluster ends a Slack message is sent with the content of a kibana.yml file ready to use. If you copy the content of the Slack message into a config/kibana.dev.yml file. There is a another way to get the kibana.yml file, you can use the oblt-cli tool to get the kibana.yml file from the Vault.

oblt-cli cluster secrets kibana-config --cluster-name=my-cluster --output-file ${PWD}/kibana.yml

you have to check that there is no configuration settings which have been duplicated or are obsolete. If you find obsolete settings please report them by creating an issue in oblt test environments repo.

After save your kibana.yml file you can start your local Kibana instance from code by using the following commands:

  • if your source code is new
NODE_OPTIONS=" --max-old-space-size=4096"
FORCE_COLOR=1
nvm install $(cat .node-version)
yarn kbn bootstrap
yarn start
  • If you had already ran the yarn kbn bootstrap
NODE_OPTIONS=" --max-old-space-size=4096" yarn start
  • If you do not need to run Kibana from code you can use the Kibana Docker image that matches with the Elastic Stack version in the oblt cluster.
docker run -it -v ${HOME}/kibana.yml:/usr/share/kibana/config/kibana.yml -p 5601:5601 docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT

docs.title: Post-configuration
docs.text: There are some settings that is not possible to set in the kibana.yml file, for those cases we need to make manual operations to configure those settings (automatic way is WIP).

docs.title: Logs and Metrics
docs.text:
Logs and Metrics need a couple of settings to use CCS: * Go to the Inventory page under Infrastructure and click on Settings in the upper right hand corner of the screen. Change the Metric indices to the the value of:

remote_cluster:metricbeat-*,metricbeat-*,remote_cluster:metrics-*,metrics-*
  • Go to the Stream page under Logs and click on Settings in the upper right hand corner of the screen. Change the Log indices to the the value of:
remote_cluster:filebeat-*,remote_cluster:logs-*,remote_cluster:kibana_sample_data_logs*,filebeat-*,kibana_sample_data_logs*,logs-*

It is possible to use oblt-cli bootstrap feature to configure the settings for Logs and Metrics:

ADMIN_PASSWORD=changeme
oblt-cli bootstrap kibana \
--verbose \
--bootstrap-folder "${HOME}/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/deployments/bootstrap-ccs" \
--username admin \
--password ${ADMIN_PASSWORD} \
--url http://localhost:5601

Note

When you use the template ess-overview there are four remote clusters configured. You will have to replace remote_cluster: with metrics-*: in the metrics settings and logs-*: in the logs settings.

If you are using the ess-overview template you can use the following command to configure the settings:

ADMIN_PASSWORD=changeme
oblt-cli bootstrap kibana \
--verbose \
--bootstrap-folder "${HOME}/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/deployments/bootstrap-ess-overview" \
--username admin \
--password ${ADMIN_PASSWORD} \
--url http://localhost:5601

docs.title: Security Solution
docs.text:
Security Solution needs some additional settings to use CCS:

In Kibana, navigate to Stack Management and in the Kibana section select Advanced Settings. If you select Security Solution in the Category drop down you will be able to quickly find the entries that need to be updated.

Duplicate the entries in the Elasticsearch indices field, securitySolution:defaultIndex, and prefix each of them with remote_cluster:.

Repeat the steps with the Threat indices field, securitySolution:defaultThreatIndex. Duplicate the entries and prefix each of them with remote_cluster:.

Select Data Views in the Kibana section of Stack Management and create data views for the following index patterns.

  • remote_cluster:auditbeat-*
  • remote_cluster:filebeat-*
  • remote_cluster:logs-*
  • remote_cluster:metrics-*
  • remote_cluster:packetbeat-*
  • remote_cluster:winlogbeat-*

It is possible to use oblt-cli bootstrap feature to configure the settings for security solution:

CLUSTER_NAME=edge-oblt-ccs-ohcnl
ADMIN_PASSWORD=changeme
oblt-cli bootstrap kibana \
--verbose \
--bootstrap-folder "${HOME}/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/deployments/bootstrap-ccs" \
--username admin \
--password ${ADMIN_PASSWORD} \
--url http://localhost:5601

For additional details please refer to the Security Solution tool documentation https://github.com/elastic/security-team/blob/main/tools/oblt-ccs-configuration.md.

docs.title: Serverless
docs.text:
We can also use serverless deployments to connect our development local Kibana instance. To do that we need to create a new serverless deployment with oblt-cli tool, then generate a config/kibana.serverless.yml file with the proper values at elasticsearch.hosts, elasticsearch.serviceAccountToken, xpack.encryptedSavedObjects.encryptionKey, xpack.security.encryptionKey, and xpack.reporting.encryptionKey settings from the credentials of the cluster.

Warning

We will run two Kibana instances against the same Elasticsearch cluster, so we need to have special care to not create objects in both instances. Create objects in both can cause versions conflicts and other issues. In case or issues, deleting the .kibana* indices in the Elasticsearch cluster can help.

  • Create a serverless cluster
  • Get credentials cluster
  • Use the oblt-cli tool to generate the kibana.yml file by connecting to the MKI k8s cluster to retrieve some settings from the Kibana configuration.
KIBANA_SRC_FOLDER="${HOME}/src/kibana"
KIBANA_YML=${KIBANA_SRC_FOLDER}/config/kibana.serverless.yaml
CLUSTER_NAME=serverless-my-cluster
ENVIRONMENT=staging

oblt-cli services mki generate-kibana-yaml \
--cluster-name "${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
--environment "${ENVIRONMENT}" \
--kibana-yaml-path "${KIBANA_YML}"

elasticsearch.hosts: https://serverless-my-cluster-fffe89.es.us-east-1.aws.staging.elastic.cloud
elasticsearch.serviceAccountToken: ABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABABA
elasticsearch.ssl.verificationMode: none
elasticsearch.ignoreVersionMismatch: true
migrations:
skip: true
server.host: 0.0.0.0
xpack:
reporting:
encryptionKey: FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF
security:
authc:
providers:
basic:
cloud-basic:
order: 150
encryptionKey: CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC
loginAssistanceMessage: 'Credentials: elastic / MyEsPaSsWoRd'
encryptedSavedObjects:
encryptionKey: DDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDDD
  • Start your local Kibana instance
KIBANA_SRC_FOLDER="${HOME}/src/kibana"
KIBANA_YML=${KIBANA_SRC_FOLDER}/config/kibana.serverless.yaml
NODE_OPTIONS=" --max-old-space-size=4096"

yarn start --serverless=oblt --config="${KIBANA_YML}"

docs.title: Manual deployments
docs.text:
It is possible to generate the configuration files and tun the Ansible playbook locally. To do that, you can use the --dry-run flag, this flag will generate the cluster configuration files but oblt-cli will not push the changes to the repository so the CI automation is not trigger.

> oblt-cli cluster create ccs --remote-cluster edge-oblt --dry-run
2021/10/04 10:39:05 Root Args: [/var/folders/rd/4lpsq9tn2h35ty0nkl9g4hlh0000gn/T/go-build4180287750/b001/exe/main cluster create ccs --remote-cluster edge-oblt --dry-run]
2021/10/04 10:39:05 Using config file: /home/my-user/.oblt-cli/config.yaml
2021/10/04 10:39:05 Reading cluster config edge-oblt-nwxrh-ccs
2021/10/04 10:39:05 Writing cluster configuration file /home/my-user/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/environments/home/my-user/edge-oblt-nwxrh-ccs.yml
2021/10/04 10:39:05 Template parameters: map[ClusterName:edge-oblt-nwxrh-ccs Date:2021-10-04T10:39:05+0200 ElasticsearchDockerImage:docker.elastic.co/cloud-ci/elasticsearch:8.0.0-alpha2-1b2647e3 RemoteClusterName:edge-oblt Seed:0x1369780 SlackChannel:@UCKPL50JY StackVersion:8.0.0-alpha1 User:inifc]
2021/10/04 10:39:05 git.add
2021/10/04 10:39:05
2021/10/04 10:39:05 git.commit
2021/10/04 10:39:05 [master 1b87c11] oblt-cli: Create a CCS cluster for inifc
 1 file changed, 46 insertions(+)
create mode 100644 environments/home/my-user/edge-oblt-nwxrh-ccs.yml

Once the file is generated /home/my-user/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/environments/home/my-user/edge-oblt-nwxrh-ccs.yml you can use it to make the deploy.

CLUSTER_CONFIG_FILE=/home/my-user/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/environments/home/my-user/edge-oblt-nwxrh-ccs.yml \
make -C ansible create-cluster

docs.title: Run in Docker
docs.text:
Oblt-cli is also available in a Docker image, the Docker image makes git checkouts from GitHub by SSH, so it needs you SSH key (-v ${HOME}/.ssh/id_rsa:/home/nonroot/.ssh/id_rsa, -v ${HOME}/.ssh/id_rsa.pub:/home/nonroot/.ssh/id_rsa.pub), your GitHub author name (-e GIT_AUTHOR_NAME="$(git config user.name)", -e GIT_COMMITTER_NAME="$(git config user.name)"), and your email (-e GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL="$(git config user.email)", -e GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL="$(git config user.email)"). I we want to persist the oblt-cli configuration, we will need to pass also a configuration line as volume (-v ${HOME}/.oblt-cli/config.yaml:/home/nonroot/.oblt-cli/config.yaml).

docker --version
touch "${HOME}/.oblt-cli/config.yaml"

docker run -it --rm \
-e GIT_AUTHOR_NAME="$(git config user.name)" \
-e GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL="$(git config user.email)" \
-e GIT_COMMITTER_NAME="$(git config user.name)" \
-e GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL="$(git config user.email)" \
-v "${HOME}/.oblt-cli/config.yaml:/home/nonroot/.oblt-cli/config.yaml" \
-v "${HOME}/.ssh/id_rsa:/home/nonroot/.ssh/id_rsa" \
-v "${HOME}/.ssh/id_rsa.pub:/home/nonroot/.ssh/id_rsa.pub" \
docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/oblt-cli:latest --help

NOTE This image is in our Docker registry in a private namespace, so it require authentication, for more details check Accessing the Docker Registry

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The oblt-cli templates are a way to create many clusters with a similar configuration. The templates are Go template files that are rendered by the oblt-cli tool. Each template is a cluster configuration file using the oblt-cli cluster configuration format, and some Go variable substitutions. The templates are stored in environment/users and have the extension .yml.tmpl. A template must have a name template_name and a description template_description. The description should explain the parameters you need to pass to the template. There are some parameters introduced by the tool automatically, they cannot be overwritten, These parameters are:

  • ClusterName: autogenerated name for the new cluster.
  • Date: timestamp of the operation.
  • SlackChannel: Slack channel configured for the tool to report the secrets.
  • User: the username to tag the deploys.

To deploy a template we will use the operation oblt-cli cluster create custom something like the following command:

oblt-cli cluster create custom --template ccs \
--cluster-name-prefix 'prefix' \
--parameters '{
    "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/cloud-ci/elasticsearch:8.0.0-alpha2-1b2647e3",
    "RemoteClusterName":"edge-oblt",
    "StackVersion":"8.0.0-alpha1"
    }'

this command uses the ccs template directly instead the cluster create ccs command.

To list the available templates you can use the oblt-cli cluster templates command:

> oblt-cli cluster templates
2023/03/30 10:34:23 Using config file: /home/my-user/.oblt-cli/config.yaml
2023/03/30 10:34:25 Already up to date.
+-------+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| LEVEL |                                    MESSAGE                                     |
+-------+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| INFO  | Profiling is deployed on release-oblt, dev-oblt, edge-oblt, and edge-lite-oblt |
|       |                                                                                |
+-------+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
📦 [autopilot] -> /home/my-user/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/environments/users/autopilot.yml.tmpl
Deploy The Elastic Stack on ESS and GKE Autopilot clusters to test the Elastic Agent on it.
Parameters:
  * ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (edge-oblt-abpag-ccs, edge-oblt-etagt-ccs)(automatic).
  * StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  * AutopilotVersion: GKE Autopilot version used for the new cluster (e.g 1.24.9-gke.3200).
  * AutopilotChannel: GKE Autopilot channel used for the new cluster (e.g. regular, rapid, stable).
  * DeployMode: Deployment mode for Elastic Agent (e.g. managed, standalone)(default: managed).
  * SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
* ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is a SNAPSHOT (docker.elastic.co/staging/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-beta1-d3622a20, docker.elastic.co/cloud-release/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT)(Optional).
  * KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana (docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT).
Usage:
  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template autopilot \
--parameters '{"StackVersion":"8.7.0", "AutopilotVersion":"1.24.9-gke.3200", "DeployMode": "standalone"}'

oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template autopilot \
--parameters '{"StackVersion":"8.7.0", "AutopilotVersion":"1.24.9-gke.3200"}'

oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template autopilot \
--parameters '{"StackVersion":"8.7.0-SNAPSHOT","ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT","KibanaDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT","ElasticAgentDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elastic-agent-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT"}'

📦 [ccs] -> /home/my-user/.oblt-cli/observability-test-environments/environments/users/ccs.yml.tmpl
Cross Cluster Search (CCS) template configures a oblt cluster that has a remote cluster configured for CCS.
Parameters:
  * ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (automatic).
  * RemoteClusterName: Oblt cluster name to configure as remote cluster (release-oblt, dev-oblt, edge-oblt, ...).
  * StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  * SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
* ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).

docs.title: Autopilot
docs.text:
Deploy The Elastic Stack on ESS and GKE Autopilot clusters to test the Elastic Agent on it. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (edge-oblt-abpag-ccs, edge-oblt-etagt-ccs)(automatic).
  • StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  • AutopilotVersion: GKE Autopilot version used for the new cluster (e.g 1.24.9-gke.3200).
  • AutopilotChannel: GKE Autopilot channel used for the new cluster (e.g. regular, rapid, stable).
  • DeployMode: Deployment mode for Elastic Agent (e.g. managed, standalone)(default: managed).
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is a SNAPSHOT (docker.elastic.co/staging/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-beta1-d3622a20, docker.elastic.co/cloud-release/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT)(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana (docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT).

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template autopilot \
--parameters '{
          "StackVersion":"8.7.0",
          "AutopilotVersion":"1.24.9-gke.3200",
          "DeployMode", "standalone
          "}'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template autopilot \
--parameters '{
          "StackVersion":"8.7.0",
          "AutopilotVersion":"1.24.9-gke.3200
          "}'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template autopilot \
--parameters '{
          "StackVersion":"8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "KibanaDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "ElasticAgentDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elastic-agent-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT"
          }'

autopilot

docs.title: CCS
docs.text:
Cross Cluster Search (CCS) template configures a oblt cluster that has a remote cluster configured for CCS. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (automatic).
  • RemoteClusterName: Oblt cluster name to configure as remote cluster (release-oblt, dev-oblt, edge-oblt, ...).
  • Target: Target environment to deploy (qa, staging, production) (default: production)
  • TrustedOrg: Trusted organization to use for CCS (Optional).
  • StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • EphemeralCluster: Whether the cluster is ephemeral (Optional)(default false).
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create ccs \
--remote-cluster=dev-oblt \
--cluster-name-prefix mycustomcluster

It is possible to use the template directly:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template ccs \
--parameters '{
          "RemoteClusterName":"dev-oblt",
          "StackVersion":"8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "Target": "qa",
          "TrustedOrg": "1176847267",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT"
          }'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template ccs \
--parameters '{
          "RemoteClusterName":"dev-oblt",
          "StackVersion":"8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "EphemeralCluster": true
          }'

docs.title: Deploy Kibana
docs.text:
Deploy Kibana template configure a oblt cluster that has a remote cluster configured for CCS and deploy a custom Kibana Docker image. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (edge-oblt-abpag-ccs, edge-oblt-etagt-ccs)(automatic).
  • RemoteClusterName: Oblt cluster name to configure as remote cluster (release-oblt, dev-oblt, edge-oblt, ...).
  • StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is a SNAPSHOT (docker.elastic.co/staging/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-beta1-d3622a20, docker.elastic.co/cloud-release/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT)(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana (docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT).
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template deploy-kibana \
--parameters '{
          "RemoteClusterName":"dev-oblt",
          "StackVersion":"8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "KibanaDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT
          "}'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template-file $(pwd)/environments/users/deploy-kibana.yml.tmpl \
--parameters '{
          "RemoteClusterName":"dev-oblt",
          "StackVersion":"8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "KibanaDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT"
          }'

deploy-kibana

docs.title: ESS
docs.text:
Elastic Cloud (ESS) template configure a oblt cluster that runs on ESS dev region on production. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (automatic).
  • StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ProductType: Type of deployment you want to make elasticsearch, observability, or security (default observability).
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • ElasticAgentDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elastic Agent. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • ZonesIntegrations: Zones to deploy the integrations server (Optional).
  • ZonesKibana: Zones to deploy the Kibana server (Optional).
  • ZonesElasticsearch: Zones to deploy the Elasticsearch server (Optional).
  • ElasticsearchMemory: Memory for the Elasticsearch server (Optional).
  • KibanaMemory: Memory for the Kibana server (Optional).
  • IntegrationsMemory: Memory for the Integrations server (Optional).

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template ess \
--parameters '{
        "StackVersion": "8.15.0",
        "ProductType": "elasticsearch"
        }'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template ess \

docs.title: Opbeans
docs.text:
Deploy The Elastic Stack on ESS and all Opbeans in a Kubernetes cluster. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (edge-oblt-abpag-ccs, edge-oblt-etagt-ccs)(automatic).
  • StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is a SNAPSHOT (docker.elastic.co/staging/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-beta1-d3622a20, docker.elastic.co/cloud-release/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT)(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana (docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT).
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template opbeans \
--parameters '{"StackVersion":"8.3.0"}'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template opbeans \
--parameters '{
          "StackVersion":"8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",
          "KibanaDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-cloud:8.3.0-SNAPSHOT",

docs.title: Serverless
docs.text:
Serverless template configure a oblt cluster that runs on Elastic Stack serverless on the QA environment. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (automatic).
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ProjectType: Type of project to deploy (elasticsearch, observability, security) (default: observability)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • FleettDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elastic Agent. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • Target: Target environment to deploy (qa, staging, production) (default: qa)
  • UpdateMode: Update mode for the cluster (default: recreate)
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).

--template serverless

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \

docs.title: APM Soak
docs.text:
Apm Soak template configure a oblt cluster that runs on Elastic Stack serverless on the selected environment. It deploys the APM Soak testing on a k8s cluster and run the selected scenario.

Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (automatic).
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ProjectType: Type of project to deploy (elasticsearch, observability, security) (default: observability)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • FleetDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elastic Agent. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • Target: Target environment to deploy (qa, staging, production) (default: qa)
  • EphemeralCluster: Whether the cluster is ephemeral (Optional)(default false).
  • ApmScenario: APM scenario to use for the cluster (Optional)(default qa).
  • ApmPerfImage: APM Perf docker image (Optional)(default docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/apm-perf:latest-1695914783-e98c86d).
  • MKICluster: MKI cluster to use for the cluster (Optional).
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).

Usage:

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{"ApmScenario": "qa"}'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{
          "ApmScenario": "qa",
          "MKICluster": 'qa-awsuse1-cp-internal-app-1'
          }'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{
          "ApmScenario": "qa",
          "ApmPerfImage": 'docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/apm-perf:latest'
          }'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{"Target": "qa"}'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{
          "FleetDockerImage": "docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elastic-agent-serverless:8.10.0-edd09abf"
          }'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{
          "KibanaDockerImage": "docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-serverless:8.10.0-edd09abf"
          }'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage": "docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-serverless:8.10.0-edd09abf
          "}'

    oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template apm-soak \
--parameters '{
          "FleetDockerImage": "docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elastic-agent-serverless:8.10.0-edd09abf",
          "KibanaDockerImage": "docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-serverless:8.10.0-edd09abf",
          "ElasticsearchDockerImage": "docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-serverless:8.10.0-edd09abf"

docs.text:

  • ProjectType: Type of project to deploy (elasticsearch, observability, security) (default: observability)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • FleettDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elastic Agent. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • Target: Target environment to deploy (qa, staging, production) (default: qa)

  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).

The only difference to the serverless template is that apps are not installed.

docs.title: Serverless Load test
docs.text:
Serverless Load test template configure a oblt cluster that runs on Elastic Stack serverless on the production environment. Then launches a load test using opbeans-go. Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (automatic).
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • FleettDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elastic Agent. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • Target: Target environment to deploy (qa, staging, production) (default: qa)
  • NumberOfOpbeansReplicas: Number of opbeans replicas to deploy (default: 10)
  • BatchLength: Time in seconds to run each batch (default: 120)
  • RequestPerMinute: Number of requests per minute to run (default: 4000)
  • Parallelism: Number of Opbeans-loadgen pods to run in parallel (default: 10)
  • GitOps: Whether GitOps is enabled (Optional).
  • GitHubCommit: For GitOps, the Git commit that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubIssue: For GitOps, the GitHub issue that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubPullRequest: For GitOps, the GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubRepository: For GitOps, the GitHub Repository, ORG/REPO, that requested this cluster (Optional).
  • GitHubCommentId: For GitOps, the GitHub comment Id that requested this cluster (Optional).

The only difference to the serverless template is that apps are not installed.

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template serverless-load-test

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template serverless-load-test \
--parameters '{"Target": "qa"}'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template serverless-load-test \
--parameters '{
        "NumberOfOpbeansReplicas": 100,
        "BatchLength": 60,
        "RequestPerMinute": 4000,
        "Parallelism": 100
        }'

docs.title: Stack Profiling
docs.text:
Deploy The Elastic Stack on k8s with ECK, all Opbeans, and configure Stack monitoring and profiling to send data to monitoring-oblt. It is possible to turn off some components of the Stack and set custom Docker images for each component. This template is designed to monitor the performance of the Elastic Stack.

Note

CCS is not supported in this template. To replace the CCS functionality, we deploy the Opbeans to generated data.

Parameters:

  • ClusterName: Name for the new oblt cluster (edge-oblt-abpag-ccs, edge-oblt-etagt-ccs)(automatic).
  • StackVersion: Elastic Stack version used for the new cluster.
  • SlackChannel: SlackChannel name or Slack member ID to send the credentials of the cluster (#my-channel, @MIDNUM)(automatic)
  • ElasticsearchEnabled: Enable Elasticsearch (true, false)(Optional).
  • ElasticsearchDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elasticsearch. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is a SNAPSHOT (docker.elastic.co/staging/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-beta1-d3622a20, docker.elastic.co/cloud-release/elasticsearch-cloud:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT)(Optional).
  • KibanaEnabled: Enable Kibana (true, false)(Optional).
  • KibanaDockerImage: Docker image to use for Kibana (docker.elastic.co/kibana/kibana:8.0.0-SNAPSHOT).
  • ElasticAgentEnabled: Enable Elastic Agent (true, false)(Optional).
  • ElasticAgentDockerImage: Docker image to use for Elastic Agent. This version should be the same build of the remote cluster if the version is an SNAPSHOT(Optional).
  • OpbeansEnabled: Enable Opbeans (true, false)(Optional).

Usage:

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template stack-profiling \
--parameters '{"StackVersion":"8.7.0"}'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template stack-profiling \
--parameters '{
        "StackVersion":"8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
        "ElasticsearchEnabled": true,
        "KibanaEnabled": false,
        "ElasticAgentEnabled": false,
        }'

  oblt-cli cluster create custom \
--template stack-profiling \
--parameters '{
        "StackVersion":"8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
        "ElasticsearchEnabled": true,
        "ElasticsearchDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elasticsearch-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
        "KibanaEnabled": true,
        "KibanaDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/kibana-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT",
        "ElasticAgentEnabled": true,
        "ElasticAgentDockerImage":"docker.elastic.co/observability-ci/elastic-agent-cloud:8.7.0-SNAPSHOT"
        }'

docs.title: Update oblt-cli
docs.text:
Since v2.0.30, the oblt-cli comes with a oblt-cli version command that prints out the current version of the tool so that you can check out what version you are using.

There are several ways to update oblt-cli. The easy way is to use the oblt-cli update command. You only need to provide a GitHub token with the --github-token option (see GitHub Token for oblt-cli).

The oblt-cli update command will download the latest version of oblt-cli and replace the current binary.

oblt-cli update --github-token "$(gh auth token)"

Note: macOS users will have to manually verify the binary using this command:

xattr -r -d com.apple.quarantine oblt-cli

docs.title: macOS via Homebrew
docs.text:
NOTE: Since v6.3.1

To check, later, if a newer version is available, use brew outdated oblt-cli. Should you then want to upgrade to the latest version, use brew upgrade oblt-cli.

If you cannot install it, please be sure you set the HOMEBREW_GITHUB_API_TOKEN environment variable:

export HOMEBREW_GITHUB_API_TOKEN=$(gh auth token)
brew outdated oblt-cli
brew upgrade oblt-cli

docs.title: Update oblt-cli downloading the latest release
docs.text: You can also download the latest release from the GitHub releases page and replace the current binary.

docs.title: oblt-cli
docs.text: CLI tool to operate the oblt clusters.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
CLI tool to operate the oblt clusters, it allows to create/update/destroy oblt clusters.

For more information, please visit:
* clusters docs at https://ela.st/oblt-clusters
* oblt-cli docs at https://ela.st/oblt-cli
* oblt-robot docs at https://ela.st/oblt-robot

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--branch string          Change the default branch to checkout from observability-test-environments repository. This flag is for testing new features. (default is main) (default "main")
      --config string          Config file (default is /Users/inifc/.oblt-cli/config.yaml)
      --disable-banner         If true, the system status banner will not be shown.
      --dry-run                If true, the Git changes will not commit the changes made to the Git repository.
      --experimental           If true, the experimental features will be available.
      --git-http-mode          If true oblt-cli uses HTTP to checkout the code and the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable to authenticate.
  -h, --help                   help for oblt-cli
      --output-file string     It is the absolute path to a file to save the operation output results. If the file name is '-' or 'stdout' it writes the data in the stdout.
      --save-config            If true, it forces to save the configuration in the config file.
      --slack-channel string   Slack member ID.
      --username string        Username to show in the deployments [a-z0-9] (any name identifies you in Elastic no matter, it is for tagging purposes e.g myuser).
      --verbose                If true, the logs output is more verbose.
      --wait int               it waits N minutes for the operation to finish. (default -1)

  • oblt-cli version - Command to show tool's version.

docs.title: oblt-cli cluster create ccs
docs.text: Command to create a CCS cluster.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Command to create a Cross Cluster Search cluster. This cluster configure a oblt cluster as remote cluster to use CCS.

oblt-cli cluster create ccs [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--cluster-name string          Name of the cluster we want to create. This is only supported when running on the CI to help with redeploying clusters. (Optional)
      --cluster-name-prefix string   Prefix to be prepended to the randomised cluster name. (Optional)
      --cluster-name-suffix string   Suffix to be appended to the randomised cluster name. If not present, a random seed will be used. This parameter ensures that the name of the cluster is unique, use it with caution. (Optional)
      --comment-id string            The GitHub comment ID that requested this cluster., to help with the GitOps automation (Optional)
      --commit string                The GitHub sha commit that requested this cluster, to help with the GitOps automation (Optional)

      --pull-request string          The GitHub Pull Request that requested this cluster, to help with the GitOps automation (Optional)
      --remote-cluster string        Oblt cluster to use (release-oblt, dev-oblt, edge-oblt, or a custom name) as a remote cluster for CCS, not a unique name for the cluster. (Required)
      --repo string                  The GitHub repository that requested this cluster, to help with the GitOps automation (Optional)

docs.text:
Command to create a cluster from a template.

The following example create a cluster using the 'ccs' template:

oblt-cli cluster create custom --template ccs --cluster-name-prefix 'prefix' --parameters '{ "ClusterName": "oblt", "StackVersion": "7.15.0", "SlackChannel": "#foo" }'

oblt-cli cluster create custom [flags]

      --cluster-name-suffix string   Suffix to be appended to the randomised cluster name. If not present, a random seed will be used. (Optional)
  -h, --help                         help for custom
      --parameters string            Parameters values defined in JSON '{ "var_name1": "value","var_name2": "value"}'. (Required)
      --parameters-file string       Absolute parameters JSON file path to use. It is incompatible with "parameters" (Optional)
      --template string              Template name to use, templates can be listed with 'oblt-cli cluster templates'. (Required if no template file is provided)

docs.title: oblt-cli cluster secrets activate
docs.text: Command to read the Activate local environment script secret from a cluster.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Command to read the local environment script secret from a cluster. It contains a script to initialice a shell to connect to the k8s cluster to operate with it. It will use Google Secrets Manager to read the secret, therefore accessing the secret will depend on Google Secrets Manager.This script is only created for cluster with k8s deployments. You can activate the environment by running the script ". ./activate", then you can use k8s commands.

oblt-cli cluster secrets activate [flags]

docs.title: oblt-cli completion bash
docs.text: Generate the autocompletion script for bash

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Generate the autocompletion script for the bash shell.

This script depends on the 'bash-completion' package. If it is not installed already, you can install it via your OS's package manager.

To load completions in your current shell session:

source <(oblt-cli completion bash)

To load completions for every new session, execute once:

docs.title: Linux:
docs.text: oblt-cli completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/oblt-cli

docs.title: macOS:
docs.text:
oblt-cli completion bash > $(brew --prefix)/etc/bash_completion.d/oblt-cli

You will need to start a new shell for this setup to take effect.

oblt-cli completion bash

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
-h, --help              help for bash
      --no-descriptions   disable completion descriptions

      --disable-banner         If true, the system status banner will not be shown.
      --dry-run                If true, the Git changes will not commit the changes made to the Git repository.
      --experimental           If true, the experimental features will be available.
      --git-http-mode          If true oblt-cli uses HTTP to checkout the code and the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable to authenticate.
      --output-file string     It is the absolute path to a file to save the operation output results. If the file name is '-' or 'stdout' it writes the data in the stdout.
      --save-config            If true, it forces to save the configuration in the config file.
      --slack-channel string   Slack member ID.
      --username string        Username to show in the deployments [a-z0-9] (any name identifies you in Elastic no matter, it is for tagging purposes e.g myuser).

docs.title: oblt-cli completion zsh
docs.text: Generate the autocompletion script for zsh

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Generate the autocompletion script for the zsh shell.

If shell completion is not already enabled in your environment you will need to enable it. You can execute the following once:

echo "autoload -U compinit; compinit" >> ~/.zshrc

To load completions in your current shell session:

source <(oblt-cli completion zsh)

To load completions for every new session, execute once:

docs.title: Linux:
docs.text: oblt-cli completion zsh > "${fpath[1]}/_oblt-cli"

docs.title: macOS:
docs.text:
oblt-cli completion zsh > $(brew --prefix)/share/zsh/site-functions/_oblt-cli

You will need to start a new shell for this setup to take effect.

oblt-cli completion zsh [flags]

      --no-descriptions   disable completion descriptions

docs.text:

oblt-cli packages cluster create-service [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--branch _dev                  Branch to use for the _dev integration source. (main by default)(Optional) (default "main")
      --cluster-name-prefix string   Prefix to be prepended to the randomised cluster name. (Optional)
      --cluster-name-suffix string   Suffix to be appended to the randomised cluster name. If not present, a random seed will be used. This parameter ensures that the name of the cluster is unique, use it with caution. (Optional)

      --integration string           Integration to be deployed. (Required)

docs.title: oblt-cli packages cluster create
docs.text: Command to create an oblt cluster.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Command to create an oblt cluster. With this command you can create an oblt cluster running the Elastic Stack and integrations.

oblt-cli packages cluster create [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--branch _dev                  Branch to use for the _dev integration source. (main by default)(Optional) (default "main")
      --cluster-name string          Name of the cluster we want to create. This is only supported when running on the CI to help with redeploying clusters. (Optional)
      --cluster-name-prefix string   Prefix to be prepended to the randomised cluster name. (Optional)
      --cluster-name-suffix string   Suffix to be appended to the randomised cluster name. If not present, a random seed will be used. This parameter ensures that the name of the cluster is unique, use it with caution. (Optional)
  -h, --help                         help for create
      --integration string           Integration to be deployed. (Required)
      --release                      True is the Elastic Stack version is a release. The default value is false. (Optional)
      --repository _dev              Integrations repository to use for the _dev integration source. (elastic/integrations by default)(Optional) (default "elastic/integrations")
      --stack-version string         Stack version to use for the deployment. (Required)

docs.text: Command to read the Activate local environment script secret from a cluster.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Command to read the local environment script secret from a cluster. It contains a script to initialize a shell to connect to the k8s cluster to operate with it. It will use Vault to read the secret, therefore accessing the secret will depend on Vault.This script is only created for cluster with k8s deployments. You can activate the environment by running the script ". ./activate", then you can use k8s commands.

oblt-cli packages cluster secrets activate [flags]

oblt-cli packages logs elastic-agent [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: -h, --help   help for elastic-agent

docs.title: Options inherited from parent commands
docs.text:
--branch string          Change the default branch to checkout from observability-test-environments repository. This flag is for testing new features. (default is main) (default "main")
      --cluster-name string    Name of the cluster. (Required)
      --config string          Config file (default is /Users/inifc/.oblt-cli/config.yaml)
      --disable-banner         If true, the system status banner will not be shown.
      --dry-run                If true, the Git changes will not commit the changes made to the Git repository.
      --experimental           If true, the experimental features will be available.
      --git-http-mode          If true oblt-cli uses HTTP to checkout the code and the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable to authenticate.
      --output-file string     It is the absolute path to a file to save the operation output results. If the file name is '-' or 'stdout' it writes the data in the stdout.
      --save-config            If true, it forces to save the configuration in the config file.
      --slack-channel string   Slack member ID.
      --username string        Username to show in the deployments [a-z0-9] (any name identifies you in Elastic no matter, it is for tagging purposes e.g myuser).
      --verbose                If true, the logs output is more verbose.
      --wait int               it waits N minutes for the operation to finish. (default -1)

docs.text: Command to sync a local folder from the workspace container logs folder.

Command to sync a local folder from the workspace container logs folder. The command will sync a local folder with the logs from the workspace container every 30 seconds. It uses kubectl + rsync to sync the files.

docs.title: Oblt cli services mki generate kibana yaml
oblt-cli services mki generate-kibana-yaml
docs.text: Command to generata a Kibana yaml config file to connet to a serverless project.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
Command to generata a Kibana yaml config file to connet to a serverless project. The Kibana YAML generated allows to connect a local Kibana instance to the serverless project.

The following example connect to the staging MKI environment:

oblt-cli services mki generate-kibana-yaml --cluster-name "servreless-qa-oblt" --kibana-yaml-path "${PWD}/kibana.serverless.yaml"

For more information about the MKI environment, please check https://docs.elastic.dev/mki/user-guide/cluster-access

oblt-cli services mki generate-kibana-yaml [flags]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
--cluster-name string       Name of the cluster. (Required)
  -h, --help                      help for generate-kibana-yaml
      --kibana-yaml-path string   Path to the Kibana yaml file to generate. (Required)

--branch string          Change the default branch to checkout from observability-test-environments repository. This flag is for testing new features. (default is main) (default "main")
      --config string          Config file (default is /Users/inifc/.oblt-cli/config.yaml)
      --disable-banner         If true, the system status banner will not be shown.
      --dry-run                If true, the Git changes will not commit the changes made to the Git repository.
      --experimental           If true, the experimental features will be available.
      --git-http-mode          If true oblt-cli uses HTTP to checkout the code and the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable to authenticate.
